content.problemStatement:
Chef wants to play a game of linear chess on a one-dimensional board ― an infinite row of squares numbered by positive integers. In this game, he has a pawn, which is initially at a square KKK. There are also NNN other people (numbered 111 through NNN); Chef can choose one of them to play against. For each valid iii, the iii-th player would play in the following way:


Take a pawn and place it on a square PiP_iPi​.
Repeat the following move any number of times: move the pawn from its current square PiP_iPi​ squares forward, i.e. from a square sss, this player's pawn is moved to the square s+Pis+P_is+Pi​.
If this player moves their pawn to the square with Chef's pawn, then Chef's pawn is captured and he loses the game.
Unfortunately, Chef cannot move his pawn during the game, making him an easy target for other players. Given the starting positions of all N+1N+1N+1 players, find a player who can capture Chef's pawn in the smallest number of moves or determine that no player can capture his pawn.
constraints: All values K, P1 … PN are pairwise distinct
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K — the number of other players and Chef's starting square. The second line contains N distinct integers P1, P2, …, PN — the starting squares of the other players. All numbers are positive and pairwise distinct with K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the starting square Pi of a player who can capture Chef's pawn in the minimum possible number of moves. If no player can capture Chef, output -1. If several players need the same minimum number of moves, any of them may be printed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, only players starting at 3 and 2 can reach square 6. Moves needed: (6/3)-1 = 1 for Pi=3, (6/2)-1 = 2 for Pi=2. The minimum is achieved by Pi=3, so output 3. In the second test case, no Pi divides 7, thus output -1.

solution.approach: A player starting at Pi can only visit squares that are multiples of Pi (Pi, 2·Pi, 3·Pi, …). Chef's square K is reachable iff K is a multiple of Pi, i.e., K % Pi == 0 and K > Pi (Pi != K by the statement). The number of moves needed is (K / Pi) - 1 because the pawn is already on Pi (the first multiple) and each move adds another Pi. For each test case we iterate over all Pi, keep the one with the smallest number of moves, and output its Pi. If no Pi divides K, answer is -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall (≤ 10^5 operations).
